title: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
gist: The Supreme Court held that Article 21 only protects against executive action, not legislative action. It interpreted "procedure established by law" narrowly, ruling that a citizen could be detained if the law allowed it, regardless of the law's fairness.
ruling: The Supreme Court held that Article 21 only protects against executive action, not legislative action. It interpreted "procedure established by law" narrowly, ruling that a citizen could be detained if the law allowed it, regardless of the law's fairness.
story:
The story of this case begins at the very dawn of the Indian Republic. A.K. Gopalan was a legendary Communist leader from Kerala who had spent a significant portion of his life fighting against British rule. However, even after India gained independence, he found himself in conflict with the new government due to his political ideologies. In 1950, just as the Indian Constitution was being celebrated, Gopalan was detained under the Preventive Detention Act. He had already been in prison for years, and this new law was used to extend his incarceration without a formal trial.

Gopalan decided to challenge his detention directly in the Supreme Court, making this the first major test of the newly minted Constitution. His legal team argued that his detention was unconstitutional because it violated his fundamental right to move freely (Article 19) and his right to personal liberty (Article 21). They argued that "procedure established by law" should be interpreted like the American concept of "Due Process," meaning that any law that takes away a person’s liberty must be fair, just, and reasonable.

However, the Supreme Court took a very literal and restrictive view of the law. The judges looked at the text of the Constitution and decided that as long as the Parliament had passed a valid law for detention, the courts could not question whether that law was "fair." They ruled that Article 21 was only a shield against the Executive (police or government) acting without a law; it was not a shield against the Legislature (Parliament) passing a harsh law.

In a move that shaped Indian law for decades, the Court introduced what became known as the "Isolated Islands" theory. They suggested that different Fundamental Rights were not connected. They argued that if a person was legally detained under Article 21, they could not claim they had the right to move freely under Article 19, because Article 19 only applied to "free" men. To the Court, these rights were separate silos rather than a connected whole.

While the majority of the judges ruled against Gopalan, there was one powerful voice of disagreement: Justice Fazl Ali. In his famous dissent, he argued that the rights to life, liberty, and movement were naturally linked. He warned that if the state could take away liberty through an unfair procedure, the Constitution’s promise of freedom would be hollow. Although Gopalan lost his case and remained in jail, Fazl Ali’s vision eventually won. Decades later, in 1978, the Supreme Court finally admitted its mistake, overturned the Gopalan ruling, and declared that all Fundamental Rights are interconnected, forming a "Golden Triangle" that ensures laws must always be just and reasonable.

title: Brij Bhushan v. State of Delhi
gist: Struck down the Chief Commissioner’s order for pre-censorship of a weekly journal. The Court ruled that "freedom of the press" is an essential part of freedom of speech and expression.
ruling: Struck down the Chief Commissioner’s order for pre-censorship of a weekly journal. The Court ruled that "freedom of the press" is an essential part of freedom of speech and expression.
story: This case ran parallel to Romesh Thappar. The government tried to impose pre-censorship on the journal 'Organizer' to prevent "communal disturbances." Justice Patanjali Sastri famously noted that imposing a ban on publication before it even reaches the public is a direct hit to the democratic right of free speech. This, along with Romesh Thappar's case, led the government to pass the 1st Amendment to add "Public Order" as a reasonable restriction in Article 19(2).

The Story

This case ran parallel to Romesh Thappar. The government tried to impose pre-censorship on the journal 'Organizer' to prevent "communal disturbances." Justice Patanjali Sastri famously noted that imposing a ban on publication before it even reaches the public is a direct hit to the democratic right of free speech. This, along with Romesh Thappar's case, led the government to pass the 1st Amendment to add "Public Order" as a reasonable restriction in Article 19(2).

title: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
gist: Invoked the "doctrine of severability" and established that freedom of speech and expression includes the freedom of propagation of ideas, which can only be restricted on grounds mentioned in Article 19(2).
ruling: Invoked the "doctrine of severability" and established that freedom of speech and expression includes the freedom of propagation of ideas, which can only be restricted on grounds mentioned in Article 19(2).
story: Romesh Thappar was the editor of a journal called 'Cross Roads', which was banned in Madras. Thappar challenged this ban, arguing it violated his right to free speech. The case remains a cornerstone for press freedom in India.

Invoked the "doctrine of severability" and established that freedom of speech and expression includes the freedom of propagation of ideas, which can only be restricted on grounds mentioned in Article 19(2).
The Story

Romesh Thappar was the editor of a journal called 'Cross Roads', which was banned in Madras. Thappar challenged this ban, arguing it violated his right to free speech. The case remains a cornerstone for press freedom in India.

title: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
gist: The Supreme Court ruled that Parliament's power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 includes the power to amend Fundamental Rights. It held that the word "Law" in Article 13 refers only to ordinary laws, not to Constitutional Amendments.
ruling: The Supreme Court ruled that Parliament's power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 includes the power to amend Fundamental Rights. It held that the word "Law" in Article 13 refers only to ordinary laws, not to Constitutional Amendments.
story:
Immediately after India gained independence, the government faced a monumental task: fixing the broken land system. For centuries, the Zamindari system had allowed a few powerful landlords to own vast tracts of land while the actual farmers lived in poverty. To create a more equal society, various state governments passed "Land Reform" acts to take surplus land from landlords and redistribute it to the poor. However, the landlords fought back, arguing in different High Courts that these laws violated their "Right to Property," which was a Fundamental Right at the time.

When some courts started siding with the landlords, the government realized that their social reform agenda was in danger. To fix this, Parliament passed the First Constitutional Amendment Act in 1951. This amendment added Articles 31A and 31B to the Constitution, which essentially said that land reform laws could not be challenged in court even if they violated Fundamental Rights.

Shankari Prasad, a landlord, challenged this amendment in the Supreme Court. His lawyers presented a clever technical argument based on Article 13(2), which says that the State shall not make any "law" that takes away or abridges Fundamental Rights. They argued that since a Constitutional Amendment is also a "law," Parliament had no power to use Article 368 to touch the Fundamental Rights section of the Constitution.

The Supreme Court was faced with a choice: protect the absolute nature of Fundamental Rights or allow the government to carry out social reforms. The Court ultimately sided with the Parliament. They made a critical distinction between two types of power: Ordinary Legislative Power (making everyday laws) and Constituent Power (amending the Constitution itself).

The Court ruled that the word "Law" mentioned in Article 13 only referred to ordinary rules and regulations. It did not include amendments made under Article 368. Therefore, the Court concluded that Parliament had the supreme power to amend any part of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights, to meet the needs of the country. This case established a "Pro-Parliament" era that lasted for over a decade, giving the government the green light to pursue its socialist goals without being blocked by the courts.

title: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
gist: Declared caste-based reservations in educational institutions unconstitutional. It established the supremacy of Fundamental Rights (Part III) over the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP), specifically Article 46.
ruling: Declared caste-based reservations in educational institutions unconstitutional. It established the supremacy of Fundamental Rights (Part III) over the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP), specifically Article 46.
story: In 1948, the Madras government used a "Communal G.O." to reserve seats in government colleges based on caste. Champakam Dorairajan, a Brahmin woman, was denied admission to medical college despite having high marks because her community's quota was already full. She challenged this as a violation of her right to equality. The SC's decision to strike down the order led to the 1st Constitutional Amendment (1951), which added Article 15(4) to empower the State to make special provisions for backward classes.

title: State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar
gist: Struck down a provision of the West Bengal Special Courts Act that allowed the State to refer any case to a Special Court. The Court established the "Intelligible Differentia" test for equality.
ruling: Struck down a provision of the West Bengal Special Courts Act that allowed the State to refer any case to a Special Court. The Court established the "Intelligible Differentia" test for equality.
story: The West Bengal government created special courts to speed up trials for "certain cases." Anwar Ali Sarkar was tried under this Act. The SC ruled that the State cannot pick and choose cases for special trials without a rational reason. This case laid the foundation for the modern interpretation of Article 14—that any classification made by the law must have a clear logic and a clear goal (nexus).

title: In Re: Berubari Union and Exchange of Enclaves
gist: The Supreme Court ruled that the Preamble is not a part of the Constitution. It also held that while Article 3 allows the government to reorganize states internally, it does not give Parliament the power to cede (give away) Indian territory to a foreign power. To do that, a formal Constitutional Amendment under Article 368 is mandatory.
ruling: The Supreme Court ruled that the Preamble is not a part of the Constitution. It also held that while Article 3 allows the government to reorganize states internally, it does not give Parliament the power to cede (give away) Indian territory to a foreign power. To do that, a formal Constitutional Amendment under Article 368 is mandatory.
story:
The roots of this case go back to the chaotic partition of India and Pakistan in 1947. When Sir Cyril Radcliffe drew the borders, he left behind several "enclaves"—tiny islands of Indian territory located entirely inside Pakistan, and vice-versa. This created a massive administrative nightmare. One such area was Berubari Union No. 12, located in West Bengal. Due to an error in the description of the boundary, both India and Pakistan claimed it. To resolve these lingering border disputes, the Prime Ministers of both countries—Jawaharlal Nehru and Feroz Khan Noon—signed the Nehru-Noon Agreement in 1958, agreeing to divide Berubari Union and exchange several other enclaves.

However, when the plan to hand over half of Berubari to Pakistan became public, it triggered massive protests in West Bengal. People were furious that the government was giving away land where Indian citizens lived without their consent. The political pressure became so intense that the President of India eventually used his power under Article 143 to ask the Supreme Court for its opinion on whether the government had the legal authority to just "give away" land through an ordinary executive decision or a simple law.

The government’s lawyers argued that Article 3 of the Constitution (which allows Parliament to change the boundaries of any state) was enough to settle the border. They also pointed to the Preamble, arguing that the "Sovereignty" of India implied the government had the inherent power to manage its territory as it saw fit.

The Supreme Court, however, disagreed with the government’s broad interpretation. First, they addressed the Preamble, famously stating that while it is a "key to open the minds of the makers," it is not a part of the Constitution itself and therefore cannot grant any specific powers to the government. Second, the Court clarified that Article 3 was meant only for internal reorganization—like splitting one Indian state into two. It was never intended to be used to slice off a piece of India and hand it to a foreign nation.

The Court concluded that if the government wanted to give away even an inch of Indian soil, it couldn't do it through a simple executive agreement or an ordinary law. It required the highest level of legal authority: a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368. This ruling forced the government to pass the 9th Constitutional Amendment Act to legally implement the agreement. This case remains a vital reminder that the territory of India belongs to the Constitution, and the government is merely its custodian.

title: Sardar Syedna Taher Saifuddin Saheb v. State of Bombay
gist: The Court struck down the Bombay Prevention of Excommunication Act. It ruled that the head of a religious community (the Dawoodi Bohras) has the power to excommunicate members as part of their right to manage religious affairs.
ruling: The Court struck down the Bombay Prevention of Excommunication Act. It ruled that the head of a religious community (the Dawoodi Bohras) has the power to excommunicate members as part of their right to manage religious affairs.
story: The Syedna, the religious leader of the Dawoodi Bohra community, held the power to "outcast" members, which meant they couldn't use the community's mosques or burial grounds. The Bombay government tried to ban this practice, calling it a violation of individual civil rights. However, the Court at the time felt that the "integrity of the religion" was more important. They ruled that if the leader couldn't enforce discipline through excommunication, the religious denomination itself might lose its identity. This remains a controversial debate between collective religious rights and individual liberty.

title: Kharak Singh v. State of Uttar Pradesh

Justice Subba Rao wrote a minority opinion that would become history: "The right to privacy is an essential ingredient of personal liberty." It took 54 years for his vision to finally become law in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).

title: Keshav Singh v. Speaker, Vidhan Sabha
gist: The Court ruled that the Judiciary has the power to review the "Privileges" of the Legislature. A citizen imprisoned by a Legislative Assembly has the right to move a High Court for a writ of Habeas Corpus.
ruling: The Court ruled that the Judiciary has the power to review the "Privileges" of the Legislature. A citizen imprisoned by a Legislative Assembly has the right to move a High Court for a writ of Habeas Corpus.
story: This was an epic "Clash of the Titans." The UP Assembly jailed Keshav Singh for contempt. When a High Court judge granted him bail, the Assembly was so angry they ordered the arrest of the judges! The judges then moved their own court to stop their arrest. It was a total constitutional breakdown. The President had to ask the Supreme Court to step in and act as the referee. The Court ruled that while the Assembly has powers, the "Rule of Law" and the High Court’s power to protect liberty are even higher.

title: Sajjan Singh v. State of Rajasthan
gist: The Court reaffirmed the logic of the Shankari Prasad case, ruling that Article 368 gives Parliament the authority to modify any part of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights. It held that an amendment is not a "law" under Article 13.
ruling: The Court reaffirmed the logic of the Shankari Prasad case, ruling that Article 368 gives Parliament the authority to modify any part of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights. It held that an amendment is not a "law" under Article 13.
story:
By the mid-1960s, the battle between land reforms and property rights was still raging. To ensure that these reforms weren't struck down by the courts, Parliament passed the 17th Constitutional Amendment Act in 1964. This amendment was a massive move—it added 44 different state laws to the 9th Schedule. The 9th Schedule was like a "legal vault"; once a law was placed inside it, it became immune to being challenged in court for violating Fundamental Rights.

Sajjan Singh, along with several other landowners, challenged the validity of this 17th Amendment. They argued that because the amendment took away the jurisdiction of the High Courts, it should have required the consent of at least half of the Indian States before being passed. They also tried to revive the argument that Fundamental Rights were "sacrosanct" and should be beyond the reach of Parliament’s amending power.

The majority of the five-judge bench was not yet ready to change the status quo. Following the precedent set 14 years earlier in Shankari Prasad, they ruled that the Parliament’s power to amend the Constitution was near-limitless. They reiterated that "Amendment" and "Ordinary Law" were two different things, and Article 13 only restricted ordinary laws from touching Fundamental Rights.

However, the real historical significance of Sajjan Singh lies in the "dissenting sparks" from two judges, Justice Hidayatullah and Justice Mudholkar. While they agreed with the final decision for technical reasons, they expressed profound "doubts" about the majority's logic. Justice Hidayatullah famously questioned if Fundamental Rights were intended to be "playthings" of the majority in Parliament.

Even more significantly, Justice Mudholkar wondered if the Constitution had certain "Basic Features" that were so fundamental that even a unanimous Parliament could not change them. He used the analogy of a building—you can renovate the rooms, but you cannot remove the foundation and still call it the same building. These small expressions of doubt were the first seeds of what would later grow into the "Basic Structure Doctrine." This case signaled that the era of the Court giving Parliament a "blank check" was coming to an end, paving the way for the explosive Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati cases that followed.

This case signaled that the era of the Court giving Parliament a "blank check" was coming to an end, paving the way for the explosive Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati cases that followed.

title: I.C. Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
gist: In a stunning reversal, the Court overruled its own previous decisions (Shankari Prasad and Sajjan Singh). It held that Fundamental Rights are "transcendental" and "immutable," meaning Parliament has no power to take them away or abridge them. The Court ruled that a Constitutional Amendment is indeed a "law" under Article 13(2), and if it violates Fundamental Rights, it is void.
ruling: In a stunning reversal, the Court overruled its own previous decisions (Shankari Prasad and Sajjan Singh). It held that Fundamental Rights are "transcendental" and "immutable," meaning Parliament has no power to take them away or abridge them. The Court ruled that a Constitutional Amendment is indeed a "law" under Article 13(2), and if it violates Fundamental Rights, it is void.
story:
The saga began in Punjab with the Golak Nath family. Henry and William Golak Nath owned a massive estate of over 500 acres. However, under the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act, the state government informed the brothers that they could only keep about 30 acres each. The rest was declared "surplus" and was to be taken over by the government. The brothers were devastated; they saw this not just as a loss of land, but as a direct theft of their Fundamental Right to acquire and hold property (which was still a protected right under Article 19 at the time).

They took their fight all the way to the Supreme Court. Recognizing the gravity of the issue—whether the Parliament was truly "supreme" over the Constitution—the Court formed a massive 11-judge bench, the largest in its history up to that point.

In a 6:5 "shocker" verdict, Chief Justice K. Subba Rao delivered a judgment that sent tremors through the halls of power. He declared that Fundamental Rights were not just ordinary legal rights; they were "Transcendental"—meaning they were above the reach of any political majority. The Court corrected its earlier mistakes and ruled that the word "law" in Article 13(2) did include Constitutional Amendments. Therefore, if the Parliament tried to use Article 368 to cut down a Fundamental Right, the Court would strike it down.

But this created a massive practical problem: if the Court declared that Parliament never had the power to amend rights, then every land reform law passed since 1950 would suddenly become illegal, leading to total chaos. To prevent this, Justice Subba Rao introduced a clever legal trick called the "Doctrine of Prospective Overruling." He basically said, "Everything Parliament did in the past remains valid to keep the peace, but from this day forward, you cannot touch the Fundamental Rights."

This ruling was a declaration of war against the government’s socialist agenda. It stripped Prime Minister Indira Gandhi of her primary tool for social engineering—the power to amend the Constitution at will. This set the stage for a decade of bitter "tit-for-tat" battles, where the government would pass a law, the Court would strike it down, and the government would then change the law again. It was the era of Golak Nath that ultimately forced the question: Who is the final boss of the Indian Democracy—the People's Parliament or the People's Constitution?

title: R.C. Cooper v. Union of India
gist: The Supreme Court struck down the Banking Companies (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertakings) Act, 1969. The Court ruled that a law must be judged by its "effect" on fundamental rights, not just its "object" or form. It also held that "compensation" for acquired property must be a just equivalent, not an arbitrary amount.
ruling: The Supreme Court struck down the Banking Companies (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertakings) Act, 1969. The Court ruled that a law must be judged by its "effect" on fundamental rights, not just its "object" or form. It also held that "compensation" for acquired property must be a just equivalent, not an arbitrary amount.
story:
In 1969, India was in the midst of a massive political shift. Prime Minister Indira Gandhi was looking to consolidate her power and push a radical socialist agenda to help the poor. In a lightning-fast move, her government issued an ordinance to nationalize 14 of India’s largest commercial banks. These banks controlled over 70% of the country’s deposits. The government’s goal was to shift credit away from big industrialists and toward farmers and small businesses. However, the move was also seen as a political masterstroke to outmaneuver her rivals within the Congress party.

Rustom Cavasjee (R.C.) Cooper, who was a director and a shareholder in the Central Bank of India, decided to challenge this move. His argument was simple: the government was seizing private property without providing "just" compensation and was preventing him from exercising his right to carry on a business. The government, relying on the old logic from the A.K. Gopalan case, argued that since the banks were taken under Article 31 (Right to Property), the Court should not look at whether the move violated Article 19 (Right to Business) or Article 14 (Right to Equality). They basically said, "If we follow the procedure for one right, we don't need to worry about the others."

The Supreme Court, in an 11-judge bench, delivered a landmark 10:1 verdict that changed Indian law forever. Most importantly, the Court officially killed the "Isolated Islands" theory. It ruled that fundamental rights are not separate; they are a connected web. The Court introduced the "Effect Test," stating that it doesn't matter what the government intended to do (the object); what matters is how the law actually impacts the citizen's rights (the effect). Even if the government had the power to nationalize banks, the way they did it was found to be discriminatory because it barred those specific 14 banks from doing banking business while letting others continue.

Furthermore, the Court ruled that the "compensation" guaranteed by the Constitution couldn't be a random or "illusory" amount; it had to be based on sound legal principles. This ruling was a massive blow to Indira Gandhi. She was so furious that she eventually pushed through the 25th Constitutional Amendment to bypass this judgment, removing the word "compensation" and replacing it with "amount" to give the government more power over private property. This case was the final spark that led to the ultimate showdown in the Kesavananda Bharati case just three years later.

title: U.N.R. Rao v. Indira Gandhi
gist: The Court held that even if the Lok Sabha is dissolved, the Council of Ministers (the Cabinet) does not cease to exist. The President cannot exercise executive power without the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers.
ruling: The Court held that even if the Lok Sabha is dissolved, the Council of Ministers (the Cabinet) does not cease to exist. The President cannot exercise executive power without the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers.
story: When Indira Gandhi dissolved the Lok Sabha in 1971 and called for early elections, U.N.R. Rao challenged her right to remain Prime Minister. He argued that since the House was dissolved, she was no longer "responsible" to the House and should step down. The Court disagreed, noting that India is a Parliamentary democracy. If there were no Cabinet, the President would become a "dictator" or a "constitutional vacuum" would be created. The Cabinet must stay to "advise" until a new one is sworn in.

title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
gist: The Court introduced the "Basic Structure Doctrine." It ruled that while Parliament has the power to amend any part of the Constitution (including Fundamental Rights), it does not have the power to destroy its "Basic Structure." If an amendment touches the core identity of the Constitution—like secularism, democracy, or the independence of the judiciary—it is unconstitutional.
ruling: The Court introduced the "Basic Structure Doctrine." It ruled that while Parliament has the power to amend any part of the Constitution (including Fundamental Rights), it does not have the power to destroy its "Basic Structure." If an amendment touches the core identity of the Constitution—like secularism, democracy, or the independence of the judiciary—it is unconstitutional.
story:
The story began not in a high-powered political office, but in a peaceful monastery in Kerala. Swami Kesavananda Bharati, the young head of the Edneer Matha, found his religious institution’s land being seized by the Kerala government under land reform acts. He felt this was a direct violation of his right to manage religious property (Articles 25 and 26). However, by the time his case reached the Supreme Court, it had evolved into something much bigger than a land dispute. It became a fight for the very soul of Indian democracy.

Following the Golak Nath ruling (which said Parliament couldn't touch Fundamental Rights), Prime Minister Indira Gandhi’s government had struck back by passing the 24th, 25th, and 29th Amendments. These amendments were designed to give Parliament absolute, "unlimited" power to change any part of the Constitution. The question before the Court was simple but terrifying: Does the Parliament own the Constitution, or does the Constitution own the Parliament?

The Court formed a 13-judge bench—the largest ever assembled in Indian history. For 68 days, the halls of the Supreme Court echoed with the brilliant arguments of the legendary lawyer Nani Palkhivala. He famously argued that if Parliament were given unlimited power to amend, they could technically use that power to abolish the Republic, end elections, or even turn India into a monarchy. He argued that the power to "amend" is the power to "improve," not the power to "destroy."

The judgment was delivered on April 24, 1973, with a razor-thin 7:6 majority. The Court came up with a brilliant compromise. It overruled Golak Nath by saying that Parliament could indeed amend Fundamental Rights. However, it added a massive "But": Parliament could not touch the "Basic Structure" of the Constitution. Like a house, you can change the paint, the windows, or the furniture, but you cannot pull out the foundation and the load-bearing walls without the whole thing collapsing.

The government was not pleased with this "checkmate." In an unprecedented move, the very next day, the government "punished" the three senior-most judges who had voted against them by bypassing (superseding) them for the post of Chief Justice. Although Swami Kesavananda Bharati technically lost his land, his case ensured that India would remain a constitutional democracy rather than a parliamentary autocracy. He never met the judges who decided his case, but he became a hero for every citizen who believes in the rule of law.

title: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
gist: The Supreme Court used the "Basic Structure" doctrine for the first time to strike down a Constitutional Amendment. It declared that Free and Fair Elections and Judicial Review are part of the basic structure of the Constitution, and therefore, the Prime Minister’s election cannot be placed beyond the reach of the law.
ruling: The Supreme Court used the "Basic Structure" doctrine for the first time to strike down a Constitutional Amendment. It declared that Free and Fair Elections and Judicial Review are part of the basic structure of the Constitution, and therefore, the Prime Minister’s election cannot be placed beyond the reach of the law.
story:
The story began with a political rivalry that changed India forever. In the 1971 General Elections, Prime Minister Indira Gandhi won a landslide victory from her constituency, Rae Bareli. Her opponent, the socialist leader Raj Narain, refused to accept the defeat. He filed an election petition in the Allahabad High Court, accusing Mrs. Gandhi of "corrupt practices"—specifically, using government officials and machinery to boost her campaign.

In 1975, in a judgment that shocked the world, Justice Jagmohan Lal Sinha of the Allahabad High Court found the Prime Minister guilty. He disqualified her from holding any elected office for six years and set aside her election. This created a massive constitutional crisis. Facing calls to resign, Mrs. Gandhi instead declared a National Emergency, suspended fundamental rights, and threw opposition leaders (including Raj Narain) into jail.

While the appeal was pending in the Supreme Court, the government used its massive majority in Parliament to pass the 39th Constitutional Amendment. They added Article 329A, which essentially said that the election of the Prime Minister and the Speaker of the Lok Sabha could not be challenged in any court of law. Clause 4 of this Article specifically stated that any past court orders (like the Allahabad High Court verdict) were now "void." It was a bold attempt to create a legal "shield" around the Prime Minister that no judge could penetrate.

When the case reached the Supreme Court, the judges were in a difficult position because the country was under Emergency rule. However, they stood their ground. They applied the brand-new "Basic Structure" rule from the Kesavananda Bharati case for the very first time. The Court ruled that Democracy requires free and fair elections, and a democracy cannot exist if the judiciary is forbidden from checking if an election was fair.

The Court struck down Clause 4 of Article 329A, declaring that Judicial Review is an essential part of the Constitution's foundation. They made it clear that even a Prime Minister is subject to the rule of law and cannot use an amendment to erase a court's power. Although the Court eventually upheld her election on different technical grounds, the legal principle was established: the Parliament might be powerful, but it is not above the "Basic Structure." This case remains a symbol of judicial courage during a time when the rest of the country’s institutions had largely fallen silent.

title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
gist: The Supreme Court established that Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not separate "islands" but form a "Golden Triangle." It ruled that any "procedure established by law" to deprive a person of their liberty must not be arbitrary; it must be Fair, Just, and Reasonable. This effectively imported the American concept of "Substantive Due Process" into India.
ruling: The Supreme Court established that Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not separate "islands" but form a "Golden Triangle." It ruled that any "procedure established by law" to deprive a person of their liberty must not be arbitrary; it must be Fair, Just, and Reasonable. This effectively imported the American concept of "Substantive Due Process" into India.
story:
The story of this case unfolded in 1977, just after the National Emergency had ended and the political tides in India had turned. Maneka Gandhi, the daughter-in-law of former PM Indira Gandhi and a journalist at the time, was issued a passport in 1976. However, in July 1977, the new Janata Party government’s Regional Passport Office sent her a letter demanding she surrender her passport within seven days "in the interest of the general public."

When Maneka Gandhi asked for the specific reasons behind this sudden move, the government refused to provide them, claiming it was not in the "interests of the general public" to disclose the details. Feeling targeted and silenced, she moved the Supreme Court, arguing that her right to travel abroad was a part of her personal liberty under Article 21, and the government’s arbitrary action violated her right to be heard.

This case forced the Supreme Court to look back at its very first decision in A.K. Gopalan (1950). For 28 years, the Court had followed the "narrow view" that if a law was written on paper, the government could follow it to take away someone’s liberty, no matter how unfair that law was. In Maneka Gandhi, the Court finally admitted that this old view was a mistake.

The 7-judge bench delivered a revolutionary verdict. They declared that Fundamental Rights are not isolated; they are interconnected in a "Golden Triangle." This means that any law taking away a person’s liberty (Article 21) must also pass the test of equality (Article 14) and the test of freedom (Article 19). The Court famously ruled that a "procedure" is not just any procedure—it must be a procedure that is "Fair, Just, and Reasonable," not "fanciful, oppressive, or arbitrary."

The Court also expanded the meaning of "Life" and "Personal Liberty." They ruled that "Life" isn't just about breathing or staying alive; it means living with human dignity. While Maneka Gandhi was eventually given the chance to explain herself to the passport authorities, the legacy of her case went much further. It opened the doors for Public Interest Litigation (PIL) and allowed the courts to protect rights that aren't even explicitly written in the Constitution, like the right to a clean environment, the right to privacy, and the right to legal aid. It turned the Supreme Court from a mere "law court" into a "justice court."

title: Minerva Mills v. Union of India

ruling: Struck down clauses of the 42nd Amendment that gave Parliament "unlimited" power. Established that a "limited amending power" is a basic feature of the Constitution.
story: During the Emergency (1975-77), the Indira Gandhi government passed the 42nd Amendment, which is often called a "Mini-Constitution." It added Section 4 and 5 to Article 368, which basically said that no constitutional amendment could be challenged in any court on any ground, and that Parliament has "unlimited" power. Minerva Mills, a textile company in Karnataka that was nationalized, challenged this. The SC struck down these sections, stating that Judicial Review is a part of the Basic Structure. The Court famously said that the Constitution is founded on a fine balance between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles, and the Parliament cannot use its amending power to destroy this balance or grant itself absolute sovereignty. This case permanently shut the door on "unlimited" parliamentary power.

title: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
gist: The Court upheld the 27% reservation for Other Backward Classes (OBCs) but set a strict 50% cap on total reservations. It also introduced the "Creamy Layer" concept to exclude the wealthy and socially advanced members of backward classes from reservation benefits.
ruling: The Court upheld the 27% reservation for Other Backward Classes (OBCs) but set a strict 50% cap on total reservations. It also introduced the "Creamy Layer" concept to exclude the wealthy and socially advanced members of backward classes from reservation benefits.
story:
The story began in the late 1970s when the Morarji Desai government appointed the Mandal Commission to identify "socially or educationally backward classes" in India. The commission's report was submitted in 1980, recommending a 27% reservation for these classes (OBCs) in government jobs. For a decade, the report gathered dust until 1990, when Prime Minister V.P. Singh suddenly announced that his government would implement its recommendations.

The announcement triggered a firestorm of protests across the country. Students took to the streets, and the nation saw tragic incidents of self-immolation. The country was deeply divided: one side argued that reservations were essential to correct centuries of caste-based oppression, while the other side argued that it would destroy the "merit-based" nature of government services. When the government later tried to add another 10% reservation for the "economically backward" among the upper castes, the legal validity of the entire reservation system was challenged in the Supreme Court.

A special 9-judge bench was formed to settle the matter. title: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
gist: The Supreme Court upheld the validity of the Anti-Defection Law. However, it struck down the provision that barred courts from interfering, ruling that the Speaker’s decision is subject to judicial review because the Speaker acts as a "Tribunal" when deciding on disqualification.
ruling: The Supreme Court upheld the validity of the Anti-Defection Law. However, it struck down the provision that barred courts from interfering, ruling that the Speaker’s decision is subject to judicial review because the Speaker acts as a "Tribunal" when deciding on disqualification.
story:
The story behind this case is rooted in a colorful but dark chapter of Indian politics known as the "Aaya Ram, Gaya Ram" culture. The phrase originated in 1967 when a Haryana MLA named Gaya Lal changed his political party three times in a single day. This constant "floor-crossing" by legislators—often in exchange for money or ministerial positions—made governments incredibly unstable and turned the mandate of the voters into a marketplace. To stop this unethical practice, Parliament passed the 52nd Constitutional Amendment in 1985, adding the 10th Schedule (the Anti-Defection Law) to the Constitution.

While the law was intended to bring stability, it contained a very controversial clause: Paragraph 7. This clause stated that the Speaker of the House had the final authority to disqualify a member for defection, and no court—not even the Supreme Court—could question the Speaker's decision. This essentially gave the Speaker "absolute power" over the fate of legislators, with no room for legal appeal.

In 1992, a group of legislators led by Kihoto Hollohan challenged the constitutionality of this law. They argued that the Anti-Defection Law violated a legislator’s right to freedom of speech inside the House. More importantly, they argued that by taking away the power of the courts to review the Speaker's decision, the Parliament had attacked the "Judicial Review" aspect of the Basic Structure of the Constitution. They pointed out that the Speaker is often a member of the ruling party, which could lead to biased decisions against opposition members.

The Supreme Court, in a landmark 3:2 split verdict, performed a delicate balancing act. The majority of the judges agreed that the Anti-Defection Law was necessary to protect the "purity of elections" and the stability of the government. However, they refused to accept that the Speaker could have the "last word" without any oversight. The Court ruled that when a Speaker decides to disqualify a member, they are acting as a Quasi-Judicial Tribunal.

Since judicial review is a "Basic Feature" of the Constitution (as established in Kesavananda Bharati), it cannot be taken away. Therefore, while the law remained valid, the Court declared Paragraph 7 unconstitutional. This meant that if a Speaker acted unfairly, maliciously, or against the rules of natural justice, the High Courts and the Supreme Court could step in to correct the error. This judgment ensured that while "Ram" might be stopped from switching parties unethically, the Speaker could not use the law as a political weapon without being answerable to the Constitution.

title: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
gist: The Court established that the power of the President to dismiss a State government under Article 356 is not absolute and is subject to judicial review. It ruled that the "Floor Test" in the Legislative Assembly is the only legal way to determine if a government has lost its majority. Crucially, it also declared that Secularism is a part of the "Basic Structure" of the Constitution.
ruling: The Court established that the power of the President to dismiss a State government under Article 356 is not absolute and is subject to judicial review. It ruled that the "Floor Test" in the Legislative Assembly is the only legal way to determine if a government has lost its majority. Crucially, it also declared that Secularism is a part of the "Basic Structure" of the Constitution.
story:
For decades after independence, Article 356 (President’s Rule) was one of the most misused provisions of the Constitution. Central governments, regardless of their party, frequently used it as a political weapon to dismiss state governments run by opposition parties. The story of this landmark case began in 1989 when the Janata Dal government in Karnataka, led by Chief Justice S.R. Bommai, was dismissed by the Governor. The Governor claimed that the government had lost its majority based on a few letters from defecting MLAs, and he refused to let Bommai prove his majority on the floor of the House.

The case eventually merged with several other similar instances, including the dismissal of BJP-led governments in Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, and Himachal Pradesh following the demolition of the Babri Masjid in 1992. The Supreme Court was faced with two massive questions: First, can the Court check if the President’s decision to impose "President's Rule" was based on honest reasons? And second, can a state government be dismissed if it acts against the principle of secularism?

In a massive 9-judge bench verdict, the Supreme Court delivered a judgment that protected the federal spirit of India. The Court ruled that the "subjective satisfaction" of the President is not beyond the law. If the Court finds that the dismissal was done for political gain or without any real breakdown of constitutional machinery, it has the power to restore the dismissed government to office. Most importantly, the Court mandated the "Floor Test"—declaring that the strength of a government must be tested on the floor of the Assembly, not in the private chambers of a Governor.

The Court also took a stand on the soul of the nation. It ruled that Secularism is a basic feature of the Indian Constitution. Therefore, if a state government promotes a particular religion or acts against secular values, it can be considered a "breakdown of constitutional machinery," justifying its dismissal. This ruling created a massive "Stop" sign for the Central government, making it extremely difficult to arbitrarily remove state leaders. It shifted India from a "Unitary-leaning" system back toward a truly Federal one, where states are not mere administrative units of the Center but are supreme within their own spheres.

title: Gian Kaur v. State of Punjab
gist: The Court ruled that the "Right to Life" does not include the "Right to Die." It upheld the constitutional validity of Section 306 (Abetment of Suicide) and effectively ruled that suicide is not a fundamental right.
ruling: The Court ruled that the "Right to Life" does not include the "Right to Die." It upheld the constitutional validity of Section 306 (Abetment of Suicide) and effectively ruled that suicide is not a fundamental right.
story: This case arose from a tragic family dispute where Gian Kaur and her husband were convicted of helping their daughter-in-law commit suicide. They argued in court that if Article 21 gives a person the right to live, it should logically include the right to not live. They essentially wanted to strike down the laws that made suicide or its assistance a crime. However, the 5-judge bench disagreed, stating that "Life" is a natural right, but "Death" is the unnatural termination of that right. The Court did, however, leave a tiny door open for "the right to die with dignity" in cases of terminal illness, which would be explored years later.

title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
gist: The Supreme Court formulated the "Vishaka Guidelines," which were mandatory rules for employers to prevent and redress sexual harassment at the workplace. The Court ruled that until Parliament passed a specific law, these guidelines would have the force of law under Article 141.
ruling: The Supreme Court formulated the "Vishaka Guidelines," which were mandatory rules for employers to prevent and redress sexual harassment at the workplace. The Court ruled that until Parliament passed a specific law, these guidelines would have the force of law under Article 141.
story:
The story behind this case is both tragic and inspiring, beginning in a small village in Rajasthan with a woman named Bhanwari Devi. She was a "Saathin"—a social worker employed by the Rajasthan government to work for the empowerment of rural women. As part of her duties, Bhanwari Devi actively campaigned against the deep-rooted practice of child marriage. In 1992, she attempted to stop the marriage of a one-year-old girl in a powerful local family.

Her intervention was met with extreme hostility. As an act of "punishment" and retribution for her activism, Bhanwari Devi was brutally gang-raped by five men from the community. When she sought justice, the system failed her miserably. The police were reluctant to investigate, and the trial court eventually acquitted the accused, even making insulting remarks about her character.

This injustice sparked a massive outcry. A group of NGOs and social activists, led by an organization called Vishaka, filed a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) in the Supreme Court. They didn't just ask for justice for Bhanwari Devi; they highlighted a systemic problem: there was no law in India that specifically addressed sexual harassment at the workplace. For a woman, going to work was a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(g), and if she wasn't safe there, her Right to Life and Dignity (Article 21) was being violated.

The Supreme Court, led by Chief Justice J.S. Verma, realized that the legislature had failed to act. In a revolutionary move, the Court turned to international law, specifically the CEDAW (Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women). They ruled that since India had signed this convention, its principles could be used to interpret Indian Fundamental Rights.

The Court declared that "Gender Equality" includes protection from sexual harassment. Since there was no domestic law, the Court performed "Judicial Legislation" by drafting a detailed set of guidelines. These included the requirement for every office to have an "Internal Complaints Committee" (ICC) headed by a woman. These Vishaka Guidelines governed Indian workplaces for 16 years until Parliament finally passed the POSH Act in 2013. This case remains a testament to how one woman's struggle for justice led to a safer environment for millions of working women across the country.

title: SCAORA v. Union of India [NJAC Case]

story: For decades, the Collegium system (judges choosing judges) was criticized for being opaque. The government tried to change this by creating the NJAC, which would include the Law Minister and "eminent persons" in the selection process. However, the Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association (SCAORA) challenged this. The Court ruled that judicial independence is a "Basic Structure" of the Constitution, and allowing politicians to have a say in judge appointments would create a "committed judiciary" rather than an independent one.

One is a permanent employee with a full salary and benefits, while the other is a "temporary" worker paid a fraction of that amount. Jagjit Singh and others challenged this blatant exploitation. The Court took a firm stand, stating that it is "fallacious" to deny equal pay simply because of the nature of the appointment. The judgment was a celebration of love and identity. Justice Indu Malhotra famously stated, "History owes an apology to the members of this community... for the delay in providing redressal." The Court ruled that sexual orientation is biological and natural, and the state has no business in the bedrooms of consenting adults.

title: Public Interest Foundation v. Union of India
gist: The Court refused to disqualify candidates from elections just because a criminal charge-sheet had been filed against them. However, it directed Parliament to make a law to curb the "criminalization of politics" and mandated that candidates must publicize their criminal records.
ruling: The Court refused to disqualify candidates from elections just because a criminal charge-sheet had been filed against them. However, it directed Parliament to make a law to curb the "criminalization of politics" and mandated that candidates must publicize their criminal records.
story: A huge percentage of Indian lawmakers have criminal cases pending against them. The Public Interest Foundation asked the Court to bar anyone with a serious charge-sheet from contesting elections. The Court faced a dilemma: a person is "innocent until proven guilty," and politicians often file fake cases against rivals. The Court decided it couldn't "write" the law (that’s Parliament’s job) but insisted on transparency so that voters could make an informed choice.

title: Romila Thapar v. Union of India

story: Five prominent activists were arrested across India on charges of being "Urban Naxals." Renowned historian Romila Thapar and others moved the Supreme Court, alleging that the arrests were a move to silence political critics. While the court didn't stop the trial, Justice D.Y. Chandrachud’s dissent became a rallying cry for activists, arguing that if you block the "safety valve" of dissent, the "pressure cooker" of the state will eventually burst.

title: CPIO, Supreme Court v. Subhash Chandra Agrawal
gist: The Court ruled that the Office of the Chief Justice of India (CJI) is a "Public Authority" and falls under the Right to Information (RTI) Act.
ruling: The Court ruled that the Office of the Chief Justice of India (CJI) is a "Public Authority" and falls under the Right to Information (RTI) Act.
story: For years, the judiciary was a "black box"—judges were exempt from the transparency rules that applied to the rest of the government. Subhash Chandra Agrawal, a persistent RTI activist, wanted to know about the assets of judges and their appointment process. The Court finally agreed that "transparency doesn't undermine judicial independence; it strengthens it." However, they added a rider: personal information of judges remains protected to maintain their privacy.

title: M. Siddiq (D) Thr Lrs v. Mahant Suresh Das & Ors
gist: In the Ayodhya Title Dispute, the Court awarded the entire 2.77 acres of disputed land to the Hindu parties for the construction of a temple and directed the government to provide 5 acres of alternative land to the Muslim parties for a mosque.
ruling: In the Ayodhya Title Dispute, the Court awarded the entire 2.77 acres of disputed land to the Hindu parties for the construction of a temple and directed the government to provide 5 acres of alternative land to the Muslim parties for a mosque.
story: This was perhaps the most anticipated and sensitive judgment in the history of the world's largest democracy. It dealt with a 16th-century mosque built over what many Hindus believe is the birthplace of Lord Ram. After a 40-day marathon hearing, the 5-judge bench used its "Extraordinary Powers" (Article 142) to bring a legal end to a dispute that had spanned centuries and caused immense communal friction, aiming for a "complete justice" that could offer closure to both communities.

title: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
gist: The Court declared that the freedom of speech and expression via the internet is a fundamental right. It ruled that indefinite internet shutdowns are unconstitutional and that any such restriction must follow the principles of proportionality.
ruling: The Court declared that the freedom of speech and expression via the internet is a fundamental right. It ruled that indefinite internet shutdowns are unconstitutional and that any such restriction must follow the principles of proportionality.
story: After the abrogation of Article 370 in Jammu & Kashmir in 2019, the government imposed a total communications blackout. Anuradha Bhasin, executive editor of The Kashmir Times, found it impossible to publish her newspaper. She moved the Court, arguing that "information is the bread and butter of a journalist." The Court’s ruling was a landmark for the digital age, asserting that while the state can restrict the internet for security, it cannot simply "pull the plug" forever without a valid, reviewable legal reason.

title: Indore Development Authority v. Manoharlal & Ors
gist: A 5-judge bench overturned the Pune Municipal Corp. ruling (Case 44). It held that land acquisition does not lapse as long as the government has tendered the payment, even if the landowner refuses to accept it.
ruling: A 5-judge bench overturned the Pune Municipal Corp. ruling (Case 44). It held that land acquisition does not lapse as long as the government has tendered the payment, even if the landowner refuses to accept it.
story: This was a rare "judicial correction." The 2014 ruling had brought hundreds of projects to a standstill. In 2020, the Court took a more state-friendly view, essentially saying that the government shouldn't be punished if a landowner "purposefully" refuses to take compensation just to stall a project. It restored clarity (and much-needed land) to the Indian state.

title: Vineeta Sharma v. Rakesh Sharma
gist: The Court ruled that daughters have equal coparcenary rights (inheritance rights) in ancestral property from birth, regardless of whether their father was alive or not when the 2005 amendment was passed.
ruling: The Court ruled that daughters have equal coparcenary rights (inheritance rights) in ancestral property from birth, regardless of whether their father was alive or not when the 2005 amendment was passed.
story: For centuries, ancestral property was the "boys' club" of Hindu families. Even after a 2005 law gave daughters rights, many courts said it only applied if the father was alive in 2005. Vineeta Sharma challenged this "technicality." Justice Arun Mishra famously wrote: "A son is a son until he gets a wife; a daughter is a daughter throughout her life." The ruling finally corrected a thousand-year-old gender bias, ensuring daughters have an "unobstructed" right to their family legacy.
